definition: (v.) To perform a task or present oneself exceptionally well; often paired as 'ate and left no crumbs.'
exampleSentence: She absolutely ate that presentation — professor was speechless.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), popularized via drag culture and social media.

definition: (adj.) Mainstream, unoriginal, or uncool; a mild insult toward someone who follows trends without originality.
exampleSentence: Let's get out of here. This party is basic.
origin: Popularized in early 2010s internet culture and hip-hop.

definition: (adj.) Authentically and unapologetically oneself, especially when holding a controversial or unpopular opinion.
exampleSentence: You brought your own snacks to the cinema? Based behavior.
origin: Originated from rapper Lil B's philosophy and spread through internet forums.

definition: (adj.) Extremely good or delicious; primarily used in the context of food.
exampleSentence: These tacos are bussin', I need another plate!
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), popularized by food-related TikTok content.

definition: (n.) A lie or exaggeration. 'Capping' means lying.
exampleSentence: He said he won the lottery, but I think he's capping.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE). 'No cap' is the negation.

definition: (adv.) An intensifier meaning 'for real' or 'honestly'; used to assert that the speaker is telling the truth.
exampleSentence: No cap, that was the best movie I've ever seen.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), popularized via hip-hop and social media.

definition: (adj.) Outdated, uncool, or trying too hard to be trendy; specifically targets aesthetics from the early 2010s.
exampleSentence: I can't believe she's still wearing skinny jeans, that's so cheugy.
origin: Coined in 2013, popularized on TikTok in 2021.

definition: (adj.) High quality, excellent, or impressive; originally rooted in cannabis culture, now applied broadly.
exampleSentence: The party last night was dank!
origin: Originally English slang for 'damp,' repurposed in cannabis culture and internet meme culture.

definition: (adj.) Over-the-top, dramatic, or excessive behavior that is often unnecessary.
exampleSentence: Her outfit for the party was extra with all the sequins and feathers.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (adj.) Amazing, impressive, or exceptionally good; synonymous with 'lit.'
exampleSentence: That new song is fire.
origin: Hip-hop slang that crossed into mainstream internet use.

definition: (adj.) Exciting, fun, or amazing; describes a high-energy situation or object.
exampleSentence: This party is lit! The music is great and everyone is having a good time.
origin: Hip-hop slang, mainstream since mid-2010s.

definition: (adj.) Mediocre, average, or underwhelming; used to describe something that fails to meet expectations.
exampleSentence: That movie was hyped up but ended up being very mid.
origin: Internet slang, popularized on Twitch and gaming communities.

definition: (adj.) Bitter, annoyed, or upset, typically over a minor or trivial matter.
exampleSentence: I lost the game to my friend. That's why I'm just a little salty about it.
origin: Popularized in gaming culture and social media in the 2010s.

definition: (adj.) Looking flawless, stylish, or physically fit; frequently used to compliment an outfit or appearance.
exampleSentence: Her outfit for the party was snatched. Everyone was talking about it.
origin: LGBTQ+ ballroom culture, popularized via drag culture and TikTok.

definition: (adj.) Short for 'suspicious'; refers to someone or something that appears sketchy or untrustworthy.
exampleSentence: Why is he acting so sus? What are you hiding?
origin: Popularized by the game Among Us (2020), but originally 90s slang.

definition: (adj.) Cool, acceptable, or logically sound; used to validate an opinion or feeling.
exampleSentence: That opinion is valid.
origin: Widespread internet slang derived from formal English validation.

definition: (adj.) Lively, bold, or flamboyant; can also refer to something dramatic or cheeky.
exampleSentence: That outfit is zesty.
origin: Internet slang, coded language in LGBTQ+ online spaces.

definition: (adj.) An absurdist adjective or filler word that can mean good, bad, cool, or evil depending on context.
exampleSentence: That's so skibidi — I don't even know what to say.
origin: Alexey Gerasimov's 'Skibidi Toilet' YouTube series — heads in toilets singing a repetitive song.

definition: (adj.) A descriptor for anything strange, weird, or cringe-worthy. References the 'Only in Ohio' meme.
exampleSentence: That is so Ohio — I cannot explain what just happened.
origin: 'Only in Ohio' meme depicting the US state as a site of supernatural or chaotic events.

definition: (n.) The act of stealing or taking a portion of a friend's food.
exampleSentence: Don't leave your fries near him — he'll apply the Fanum Tax.
origin: Streamer Kai Cenat and his friend Fanum, who frequently 'taxed' Kai's food on stream.

definition: (n.) Mental decline from consuming excessive low-quality digital content; used as a self-aware label.
standardEnglish: Excessive social media consumption / cultural decline
exampleSentence: I've been watching Skibidi Toilet for 3 hours. The brainrot is real.
origin: Concept of mental decline repurposed as an ironic, self-aware internet culture term.

definition: (adj.) Describes something or someone ridiculous, silly, or funny in an absurd way.
exampleSentence: He showed up in a goofy ahh outfit to the formal event.
origin: Phonetic variation of 'Goofy ass,' spread through meme culture.

definition: (v.) To lose control, have a meltdown, or act recklessly due to frustration.
exampleSentence: He crashed out when they canceled his favorite show.
origin: Internet vernacular describing an intense emotional outburst.

definition: (v.) An orthodontic tongue-posture technique popularized for jawline definition; also a 'no talking' gesture.
standardEnglish: Jaw-defining tongue posture exercise; signaling silence
exampleSentence: He's been mewing for six months — can't tell if it's working.
origin: Popularized by Dr. Mike Mew's orthodontic technique, became a meme signifying working on appearance.

definition: (n.) The most dominant, powerful, or assertive person in a group; sometimes used mockingly.
exampleSentence: He thinks he's the alpha of the friend group, but it's just cringe.
origin: Borrowed from evolutionary biology, popularized by internet self-improvement communities.

definition: (n.) A 'lone wolf' leader who is successful and independent, existing outside the Alpha/Beta hierarchy.
exampleSentence: He has major sigma energy — he doesn't care what anyone thinks.
origin: Evolved from 'sigma male' meme, popularized through internet self-improvement content.

definition: (n.) Someone with a high degree of 'rizz' (charisma); a top-tier charmer.
exampleSentence: He's the ultimate rizzler; he can talk to anyone.
origin: Derived from 'rizz,' popularized by streamer Kai Cenat.

definition: (n.) 'Non-Player Character' — someone who lacks originality and seems to follow a pre-set script.
standardEnglish: Someone who is boring or unthinking
exampleSentence: I felt like an NPC in that meeting, just nodding along.
origin: Gaming term for computer-controlled characters, adopted as social commentary.

definition: (n.) Short for 'Uncle'; refers to an older person or someone out of touch with modern trends.
exampleSentence: I couldn't figure out how to repost on TikTok — I feel so unc.
origin: Abbreviation of 'uncle,' used to describe generational disconnection.

definition: (n.) A stereotypically hyper-masculine, confident male; often used humorously.
exampleSentence: He's such a Chad with that workout routine.
origin: Originated in internet incel culture, widely adopted ironically.

definition: (n., v.) An obsessive or highly dedicated fan; portmanteau of 'stalker' and 'fan.'
exampleSentence: I stan Beyoncé — she's the queen of everything.
origin: From Eminem's 2000 song 'Stan,' depicting an obsessive fan.

definition: (n.) A term of endearment for a loved one or best friend.
exampleSentence: That's my pookie — we go everywhere together.
origin: General endearment term, popularized through social media affection.

definition: (n.) Short for 'opposition'; an enemy, rival, or someone the speaker dislikes.
exampleSentence: He hangs out with the opps now? Wild.
origin: Hip-hop slang that crossed into mainstream internet use.

definition: (n.) A romantic or sexual relationship that lacks clear labels or commitment; 'more than friends, less than a relationship.'
exampleSentence: We don't see other people but we aren't official… it's a situationship.
origin: Emerged from Gen Z's reluctance to commit to relationship labels, popularized on social media.

definition: (n.) A sudden, irreversible feeling of disgust toward a romantic interest triggered by a minor habit or trait.
exampleSentence: He chewed with his mouth open and I got the ick immediately.
origin: Popularized on TikTok and dating discourse, particularly from creator Olivia Neill.

definition: (v.) Suddenly and completely ending communication with someone without explanation.
exampleSentence: He ghosted me after our first date — so rude!
origin: Dating culture term from the 2010s, widely adopted in social media discourse.

definition: (v.) Subtly introducing a new partner on social media without showing their face.
standardEnglish: Subtly hinting at a new relationship on social media
exampleSentence: She posted his shoes in her story — classic soft launch.
origin: Borrowed from marketing terminology and applied to relationship reveals on social media.

definition: (n.) The definitive, public reveal of a relationship through clear social media posts.
standardEnglish: Officially announcing a relationship on social media
exampleSentence: Two weeks after the soft launch, she finally hard launched him on IG.
origin: Counterpart to 'soft launch,' borrowed from marketing terminology.

definition: (n.) Truncated from 'charisma'; refers to one's ability to charm or flirt successfully.
exampleSentence: He's got mad rizz — everyone loves him.
origin: Popularized by Twitch streamer and YouTuber Kai Cenat around 2021.

definition: (n.) A secret romantic or casual relationship kept hidden from others.
exampleSentence: They've been a sneaky link for months now.
origin: Popularized through hip-hop and social media in early 2020s.

definition: (n.) Someone who displays excessive affection or submissiveness to a crush, often without reciprocation.
exampleSentence: Stop simping for her — she's not even interested.
origin: Old English slang repopularized through Twitch streaming culture around 2019.

definition: (n.) Short for 'Win.' Used to rate an action, person, or event as a success.
exampleSentence: He got promoted and got a raise in the same week. Massive W.
origin: Gaming culture shorthand, adopted widely in social media commentary.

definition: (n.) Short for 'Loss.' Used to rate an action, person, or event as a failure.
exampleSentence: He tripped in front of everyone. That's a big L.
origin: Gaming culture shorthand, adopted widely in social media commentary.

definition: (interjection) Short for 'Good Game.' Used at the end of a match; can also be used sarcastically to mean 'it's over.'
exampleSentence: They got completely demolished in the tournament. GG.
origin: Competitive gaming culture, now used broadly as social commentary.

definition: (v.) Occurs when a reply to a post gets more likes than the original post, indicating widespread disagreement.
standardEnglish: Publicly proven wrong by community response
exampleSentence: He made a terrible take and got absolutely ratioed.
origin: Twitter/X culture term for when a reply surpasses the original post in engagement.

definition: (n.) Proof or evidence of a claim, typically in the form of screenshots or text messages.
exampleSentence: She said she never said that but I have the receipts.
origin: Popularized through celebrity drama and social media call-out culture.

definition: (n.) Fear Of Missing Out. The anxiety that others are having rewarding experiences from which one is absent.
exampleSentence: I couldn't make it to the concert and the FOMO is real.
origin: Coined by Patrick J. McGinnis in 2004, popularized through social media.

definition: (phr.) If You Know You Know. Indicates an inside joke understood only by a specific group.
standardEnglish: Inside joke / You had to be there
exampleSentence: The conference was absolutely wild. IYKYK.
origin: Internet acronym that became widely used across social media platforms.

definition: (n.) Point Of View. Used to describe a scene from a specific perspective; common in TikTok skits.
standardEnglish: Point of view / Imagine you are...
exampleSentence: POV: you're late to class and the teacher just locked the door.
origin: Film/literature term adopted by TikTok creators to frame skits.

definition: (adv.) Seriously; used to emphasize that one is telling the absolute truth.
exampleSentence: Deadass, that was the scariest thing I've ever seen.
origin: New York City slang, popularized nationally through internet culture.

definition: (phr.) Indicates that the speaker has understood the point and no further explanation is needed.
exampleSentence: Need a ride to the airport? Say less, I'll be there at 6.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), popularized via hip-hop.

definition: (n.) Social media influence, fame, or power. 'Clout chasing' means doing things for attention.
exampleSentence: He only hangs out with celebrities for the clout.
origin: Hip-hop slang for influence, adopted widely by social media culture.

definition: (n.) An aesthetic centered on simple, rural living, traditional crafts, and harmony with nature.
exampleSentence: Her whole Instagram is pure cottagecore — bread baking and wildflowers.
origin: Niche internet aesthetic community that emerged around 2018–2020.

definition: (n.) A hyper-feminine aesthetic dominated by vibrant pinks and inspired by the Barbie franchise.
exampleSentence: She showed up to the party in full Barbiecore — head-to-toe pink.
origin: Emerged in 2023 alongside the Barbie film, popularized by fashion influencers.

definition: (n.) Social media-driven alternative aesthetic featuring heavy eyeliner, dyed hair, and 90s/emo influences.
exampleSentence: He's got full e-boy vibes with the striped long-sleeve under the graphic tee.
origin: Emerged on TikTok around 2019, rooted in e-culture and emo subculture.

definition: (n.) Embracing a gentle, relaxed, aesthetically pleasing lifestyle; often a rejection of hustle culture.
exampleSentence: She's fully embraced the soft girl era — no drama, just vibes.
origin: TikTok aesthetic trend emphasizing pastel colors, femininity, and emotional openness.

definition: (phr.) A shift in wealth definition away from money toward experiences, health, and curiosity.
standardEnglish: Wealthy in experiences and fulfillment, not money
exampleSentence: I'm not making bank but I'm genuinely rich in life right now.
origin: Emerging cultural counter-narrative to material wealth definitions, 2025–2026.

definition: (adj.) Describing someone who is mentally absent, clueless, or checked out — referencing the HTTP 'Not Found' error.
exampleSentence: He had no idea what we were talking about. Fully 404 coded.
origin: Tech-savvy internet slang borrowing from HTTP error codes, emerging 2025–2026.

definition: (n.) Framing unexpected detours or minor tasks as adventures in the 'game' of real life.
standardEnglish: Unexpected but interesting detour or task
exampleSentence: Getting lost led us to the best restaurant — total side quest moment.
origin: Gaming metaphor applied to real life, popularized through life-as-game internet framing.

definition: (n.) A defining life moment — often chaotic or difficult — viewed as essential to personal development.
exampleSentence: Getting rejected from your first choice school is such a canon event.
origin: Narrative/storytelling concept applied to personal life, popularized by TikTok in 2023–2024.

definition: (v.) Performatively curating one's vibe or image to gain social 'aura points' rather than being authentic.
exampleSentence: He doesn't actually meditate — he's just aura farming for Instagram.
origin: Derived from gaming 'farming' mechanics, applied to social media performance, 2025–2026.

definition: (n.) An unapologetic, messy, and carefree aesthetic trend defined by lime-green visuals and fearlessness.
exampleSentence: She's fully in her brat summer era — not apologizing for anything.
origin: Coined by Charli XCX in 2024 with her album 'brat,' defining a cultural moment.

definition: (n.) The distinctive energy, atmosphere, or level of coolness a person exudes; actions can result in gaining or losing arbitrary aura points.
exampleSentence: Tripping on the stairs just cost you a thousand aura points.
origin: TikTok / Gen Z and Gen Alpha internet culture.

definition: (interjection) A versatile term of agreement, confirmation, or acceptance of a challenge.
exampleSentence: You want to meet at the library at eight? Bet.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), widely adopted by Gen Z and Millennials.

definition: (interjection) An acronym for "be f***ing for real," used to express extreme annoyance or disbelief at an outlandish statement.
exampleSentence: You think pineapple belongs on pizza? BFFR right now.
origin: TikTok / Gen Z internet culture.

definition: (n.) A musical track or song that is considered exceptionally good, catchy, or enjoyable.
exampleSentence: I did not expect to like her new album, but every track is a bop.
origin: Pop culture slang, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adj.) Unattractive, of exceptionally low quality, or completely failing to meet standard expectations.
exampleSentence: I am not going to that restaurant, the food looks completely chopped.
origin: NYC Drill music culture, adopted by Gen Alpha.

definition: (adj.) Completely and utterly destroyed, worn out, or physically unattractive.
exampleSentence: That old car he drives is absolutely clapped, it is falling apart.
origin: UK gaming and internet slang, adopted broadly by Gen Z.

definition: (adj.) Something that is deeply embarrassing, awkward, or painfully uncool to witness, causing sympathetic secondhand embarrassment.
exampleSentence: Watching the executives try to do TikTok dances was incredibly cringe.
origin: General internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adj.) A playful abbreviation for delusional; holding highly unrealistic, often romantic or career-based expectations.
exampleSentence: Thinking he is going to text you back after a week is peak delulu behavior.
origin: K-Pop fandom culture, adopted broadly by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) High-quality, highly fashionable clothing, jewelry, or overall personal aesthetic style.
exampleSentence: Look at his new sneakers, the drip is absolutely immaculate today.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), widely adopted by Gen Z and Gen Alpha.

definition: (v.) To handle a situation skillfully, or to manipulate a person or system to get exactly what one wants.
exampleSentence: She managed to finesse her way into the VIP section without a ticket.
origin: Hip-hop culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) To excessively and overwhelmingly praise someone to the point of embarrassing sycophancy.
exampleSentence: You need to stop glazing the manager, it is getting embarrassing.
origin: Internet culture, popularized by Gen Alpha.

definition: (n.) A significant, highly noticeable positive transformation in one's physical appearance, confidence, or lifestyle over time.
exampleSentence: Her glow up from middle school to college is truly unbelievable.
origin: Internet culture, popularized via social media by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) An acronym standing for the Greatest Of All Time, used to express ultimate superiority in a specific field.
standardEnglish: The best ever / Greatest of all time
exampleSentence: When it comes to competitive swimming, Michael Phelps is the GOAT.
origin: Sports culture, popularized by Muhammad Ali and widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) A type of behavior that is unapologetically slothful, self-indulgent, or feral, rejecting standard societal expectations.
standardEnglish: Lazy self-indulgence / Not caring about appearances
exampleSentence: I spent the entire weekend in goblin mode, eating snacks in bed.
origin: Internet culture, named Oxford Word of the Year in 2022.

definition: (interjection) An exclamation of intense surprise, excitement, or admiration, frequently referencing a highly attractive physique.
exampleSentence: Gyatt, did you see the speed on that sports car?
origin: Twitch streaming culture, popularized by Gen Alpha.

definition: (phr.) Something that is significantly better, more intense, or emotionally resonant in a peculiar or specific manner.
standardEnglish: Exceptionally good in a unique way
exampleSentence: Drinking ice cold water at three in the morning just hits different.
origin: Pop culture internet slang, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (phr.) A fill-in-the-blank formula used to describe the specific vibe, energy, or aesthetic that something or someone is radiating.
standardEnglish: It radiates the energy of / It looks like
exampleSentence: The interior design of this new cafe is really giving cozy winter cabin.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture, popularized by Gen Z via social media.

definition: (v.) A trend encouraging young men to systematically enhance their physical appearance through hygiene, fitness, or extreme measures.
exampleSentence: He has been looksmaxxing all year and completely changed his jawline.
origin: Internet culture and incel-adjacent online communities, adopted by Gen Alpha.

definition: (n.) The complex backstory, history, or contextual inside jokes surrounding a real person, friend group, or situation.
exampleSentence: You really need to understand the deep lore of our friend group to get this joke.
origin: Gaming culture, applied to real-life contexts by Gen Z.

definition: (adv.) Used to express a feeling or desire subtly, quietly, or secretly, downplaying the intensity of the emotion.
exampleSentence: I am low-key terrified for this upcoming presentation.
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) Acting like the star of one's own cinematic story; possessing high confidence, charisma, and center-stage energy.
standardEnglish: Protagonist energy / Acting like the star
exampleSentence: She walked into the crowded room radiating absolute main character energy.
origin: TikTok / Gen Z internet culture.

definition: (v.) The act of physically dominating or appearing vastly superior to someone else in physical attractiveness in a direct comparison.
exampleSentence: He stood next to his brother in the photo and was completely mogging him.
origin: Looksmaxxing internet communities, adopted by Gen Alpha.

definition: (n.) A term of highest praise for a woman or femme figure who is iconic, dominant, and deeply respected in her field.
exampleSentence: She handled that crisis management situation flawlessly, absolute Mother.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture, adopted by Gen Z via drag media.

definition: (interjection) An audible expression of sympathy, secondhand embarrassment, or acknowledgment of a painful or unfortunate situation.
exampleSentence: You dropped your entire tray of food in the cafeteria? Oof, that is rough.
origin: Roblox gaming culture, adopted broadly by Gen Z.

definition: (adj.) Acting wildly inappropriately, out of line, overstepping boundaries, or saying something highly offensive.
exampleSentence: The joke he made during the solemn meeting was completely out of pocket.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) A derogatory term for someone who seeks approval by putting others down or pretending to be uniquely different.
exampleSentence: Saying you only hang out with guys because there is less drama is peak pick me energy.
origin: TikTok / Gen Z internet culture.

definition: (v.) To perform exceptionally well, to angrily express one's feelings, or to succeed spectacularly in a given moment.
exampleSentence: Your new video just hit a million views in an hour, pop off!
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adv.) When a thought, memory, song, or person constantly occupies one's mind without any effort or invitation.
standardEnglish: Living in my head / Obsessively thinking about
exampleSentence: That catchy commercial jingle is living completely rent free in my head right now.
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) The act of passively observing gossip or drama unfold without actively participating or intervening.
exampleSentence: I am just going to sit in the corner, sip my tea, and watch this argument happen.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) / internet gossip culture.

definition: (v.) To execute a task exceptionally well, to look incredible, or to dominate a situation with high confidence.
exampleSentence: You completely slayed that corporate presentation today, they loved it.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) A person who is considered highly physically attractive and desirable.
exampleSentence: Have you seen the new barista at the coffee shop? Total snack.
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) To deliberately avoid, ignore, or move away from someone or something, particularly drama or unwanted attention.
exampleSentence: I saw my ex walking down the aisle at the grocery store and had to hard swerve.
origin: Hip-hop culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) A command telling someone to disconnect from the internet, go outside, and reconnect with physical reality.
standardEnglish: Go outside / Log off the internet
exampleSentence: You have been arguing on Twitter for six hours, you desperately need to touch grass.
origin: Internet culture, used by Gen Z and Gen Alpha to mock excessive online engagement.

definition: (adj.) Existing in a state of calm detachment; not caring about criticism, drama, or negative external factors.
exampleSentence: Despite all the negative rumors spreading, she remains completely unbothered.
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adj.) Cool, trendy, or possessing a smooth, highly appealing style or artistic aesthetic.
exampleSentence: The production on this new track is incredibly wavy.
origin: Hip-hop culture, adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (interjection) An exclamation indicating that something is so surprising, amazing, or shocking that it conceptually blew the speaker's wig off.
exampleSentence: When she hit that high note during the concert, my wig was officially snatched.
origin: Pop culture / drag culture, adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) To talk excessively, continuously, and often loudly about trivial, nonsensical, or boring topics.
exampleSentence: He has been yapping for twenty straight minutes and has not made a single point.
origin: Internet culture, adopted by Gen Z and Gen Alpha.

definition: (v.) To throw something with immense force and abandon; also used as an exclamation of extreme excitement or sudden action.
exampleSentence: He grabbed the empty soda can and immediately yeeted it across the entire field.
origin: Vine internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) A widely accepted demographic label for a member of Generation Z, acting as a portmanteau of Generation Z and Boomer.
exampleSentence: As a standard zoomer, she consumes almost all of her daily news through video applications.
origin: Internet culture / Millennials referring to Gen Z.

definition: (interjection) An exaggerated expression of shock, surprise, or absolute amazement, functioning as a playful variation of the word dang.
exampleSentence: You paid three hundred dollars for those jeans? Zang, that is expensive.
origin: Gen Alpha internet culture.

definition: (adj.) Highly aggressive, deeply agitated, or causing severe annoyance and frustration.
exampleSentence: The customer service representative was acting incredibly aggy on the phone.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (adv.) An acronym standing for "as f***," used as an intensive modifier placed after an adjective to multiply its effect.
exampleSentence: I am tired asf after working a twelve-hour shift today.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) / internet culture.

definition: (n.) A prolonged conflict, serious disagreement, or lingering hostility between two individuals or opposing groups.
exampleSentence: Those two rival artists have had serious beef spanning over a decade.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), widely used in hip-hop culture.

definition: (adv.) A tense marker used to describe a long duration of knowledge or a state of being, effectively acting as an anterior tense.
exampleSentence: You are just finding out about this now? We been knew about the situation.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) grammatical construction.

definition: (adv.) A phonetic compression of the phrase "about to," indicating an immediate future action or intent.
exampleSentence: I am boutta head out to the grocery store, do you need anything?
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (interjection) A versatile expression of disbelief, profound disappointment, or shock; a phonetic variant of "brother."
exampleSentence: You left the concert tickets sitting on the kitchen counter? Bruh.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), adopted globally by Gen Z and Gen Alpha.

definition: (interjection) A phonetic spelling of the word "child," pronounced without the terminal consonant, used to express exasperation or amusement.
exampleSentence: Chile, you will not believe the ridiculous drama that happened at work today.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (adj.) A state of profound calmness, relaxation, or easygoing nature. Lacking chill implies being overly intense or neurotic.
exampleSentence: We are not doing anything crazy tonight, we are just going to chill at home.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), widely mainstream.

definition: (n.) One's house, apartment, or primary living space. Historically rooted in Harlem slang.
exampleSentence: Let us grab some takeout food and head back to my crib to eat.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), rooted in Harlem slang.

definition: (n.) A person of exceptional physical attractiveness, acting as shorthand for a flawless ten out of ten rating.
exampleSentence: Did you see the woman who just walked into the restaurant? Absolute dime.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (v.) The sudden act of leaving a location, often rapidly or without providing a detailed explanation to the hosts.
exampleSentence: This party is getting incredibly boring, I think it is time for us to dip.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (adj.) Highly cool, excellent, impressive, or culturally relevant. Historically associated with illicit substances, now a broad compliment.
exampleSentence: That vintage leather jacket you just bought is incredibly dope.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), mainstreamed via hip-hop.

definition: (adj.) When a person is desperately at their lowest emotional, financial, or mental point; often describing romantic desperation.
exampleSentence: He called his ex-girlfriend five times in one hour, he is absolutely down bad.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adv.) A deep contraction of the phrase "fixing to," heavily utilized to denote intent or a highly impending future action.
exampleSentence: I am finna grab some fast food for lunch, I will be right back.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (adv.) Utilized strictly at the end of a sentence to add intense finality and absolute emphasis; the T indicates a hard phonetic stop.
exampleSentence: This is without a doubt the best restaurant in the entire city, periodt.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE).

definition: (n.) A term of endearment and solidarity, historically used among Black women, now utilized globally to greet friends regardless of gender.
exampleSentence: Sis, you absolutely have to tell me the details of what happened last night.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE), adopted broadly by Gen Z.

definition: (v.) To share exclusive gossip, reveal private news, or uncover hidden information regarding interpersonal drama.
standardEnglish: Share the gossip / Tell me everything
exampleSentence: Do not hold anything back, you need to spill the tea on the workplace drama.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) / Ballroom culture.

definition: (v.) To harshly critique, systematically roast, or ruthlessly make fun of someone in a public forum.
exampleSentence: The entire internet dragged the politician for hours after that terrible speech.
origin: African American Vernacular English (AAVE) / Ballroom culture.

definition: (adj.) Something that is ironically trendy, highly appealing specifically because of its bad taste, or incredibly theatrical.
exampleSentence: Wearing a massive chandelier as a functional dress to the gala is absolute peak camp.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom and Queer culture.

definition: (adj.) To be rendered entirely speechless by something incredibly impressive, deeply shocking, or exceptionally beautiful.
exampleSentence: Her unbelievable vocal performance during the finale completely gagged the audience.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture.

definition: (v.) The precise act of pointing out someone's physical or personality flaws in a witty, highly exaggerated, and rapid-fire manner.
standardEnglish: Delivering a witty insult / Calling someone out
exampleSentence: She was reading him to absolute filth over the terrible outfit he chose to wear.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture.

definition: (n.) The performance ability to convincingly pass as a specific gender, social class, or archetype during a presentation or daily life.
exampleSentence: He confidently walked right into the corporate boardroom serving pure executive realness.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture.

definition: (v.) To deliver a specific look, performance, or attitude with absolute confidence and undeniable success.
exampleSentence: She stepped out onto the red carpet and effortlessly served pure Hollywood glamour.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture, adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) A highly subtle, deeply indirect, or underhanded insult or critique aimed at undermining an opponent without explicit aggression.
exampleSentence: She did not mention his name explicitly, but she was definitely throwing heavy shade.
origin: LGBTQ+ Ballroom culture, widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (adv.) A ubiquitous submodifier utilized essentially in place of "very" or "a lot." The most famous Northern Californian slang term.
exampleSentence: The traffic on the bridge heading into the city is hella backed up today.
origin: Northern California (Hayward / Bay Area), popularized nationally.

definition: (adj.) An intensely energetic, rowdy, and wild state of mind; also a distinct subgenre of minimalist, bass-heavy hip-hop originating in Oakland.
exampleSentence: The energy in the arena got completely hyphy once the hometown rapper took the stage.
origin: Oakland / Bay Area, associated with the Hyphy movement spearheaded by E-40 and Mac Dre.

definition: (adv.) A highly versatile phonetic compression of "for sure," utilized universally as an affirmative response, greeting, or acknowledgment.
exampleSentence: Do you mind if I grab a cold drink from your refrigerator? Fasho, go ahead.
origin: Bay Area / Northern California slang.

definition: (n.) San Francisco. The only acceptable colloquial abbreviation used by native Bay Area residents, heavily rejecting terms like "San Fran."
exampleSentence: We are taking the train across the bay to spend the entire weekend in The City.
origin: SF Bay Area native vernacular.

definition: (n.) Oakland. Used to establish a distinct, proud cultural boundary separating the East Bay metropolis from San Francisco across the water.
exampleSentence: There is a massive outdoor music festival happening over in The Town tomorrow afternoon.
origin: East Bay / Oakland vernacular.

definition: (adj.) Describing a physical object, digital system, or situational plan that is of exceptionally poor quality, highly unreliable, or entirely broken.
exampleSentence: The screen on this old smartphone is completely janky, I need to buy a replacement.
origin: Northern California / Bay Area slang.

definition: (v.) Used to describe something exceptionally good or impactful. Historically applied to music with heavy bass, now applies broadly to virtually anything impressive.
exampleSentence: I have never listened to this band before, but their new album absolutely slaps.
origin: Bay Area / Northern California slang, now widely adopted across internet culture.

definition: (adj.) An adjective describing a location, situation, or individual that is hidden off the beaten path, heavily sketchy, slightly dangerous, or shady.
exampleSentence: I do not want to walk down that dark alleyway, it looks incredibly cutty.
origin: Bay Area slang, referencing "the cuts" (secluded areas).

definition: (n.) The anthropomorphized, affectionate local nickname for the dense fog that frequently engulfs San Francisco during the summer.
exampleSentence: We planned a picnic at the park, but unfortunately Karl decided to crash the party.
origin: San Francisco local culture / social media.

definition: (phr.) A rapid, phonetic compression of the phrase "Do you know what I mean?", popularized by Bay Area rapper Keak da Sneak.
standardEnglish: You know what I mean / Understand?
exampleSentence: I want to head back to the house before it gets entirely too dark out, yadadamean?
origin: SF Bay Area, popularized by rapper Keak da Sneak.

definition: (v.) Used to describe exceptionally delicious, high-quality food. In the Bay Area, music slaps but food smacks.
exampleSentence: The street tacos from that specific food truck down the block are absolutely smackin.
origin: SF Bay Area vernacular.

definition: (v.) The dangerous act of placing a vehicle in neutral, exiting the moving car, and dancing alongside or on top of it.
exampleSentence: The police cracked down heavily on people trying to ghost-ride their whips down the boulevard.
origin: Oakland Hyphy movement culture.

definition: (n.) A ubiquitous slang substitute for the word "car" or any motorized vehicle. Originally regional Bay Area slang, now proliferated across global internet culture.
exampleSentence: My parents promised they would help me buy my own whip once I finally graduate.
origin: SF Bay Area slang, now mainstream across internet culture.

definition: (adv.) A phonetic portmanteau of "trying to," but functioning semantically as a casual invitation meaning "would you like to?" or "shall we?"
standardEnglish: Want to / Shall we / Trying to
exampleSentence: I am hungry, you tryna go grab some tacos from the corner store?
origin: SF Bay Area slang.

definition: (adj.) An extreme descriptor maintaining a dual semantic nature; it can describe something incredibly impressive or incredibly dangerous and bad.
exampleSentence: The traffic collision on the highway looked incredibly gnarly, I hope everyone is okay.
origin: Southern California surf culture.

definition: (adj.) Existing in a state of supreme excitement, high anticipation, or intense forward-looking enthusiasm regarding an upcoming event or activity.
exampleSentence: I finally secured tickets to the music festival this weekend, I am completely stoked.
origin: Southern California surf culture.

definition: (n.) A deeply insulting term used to label someone as a poser, specifically someone attempting to appropriate surf or skate culture without authentic experience.
exampleSentence: He showed up to the beach carrying a brand new board in designer jeans, absolute kook.
origin: Southern California surf culture.

definition: (adj.) Describing a situation, physical environment, or specific individual that seems highly dangerous, suspicious, or projects an overwhelming bad vibe.
exampleSentence: I do not trust that abandoned building, the entire area seems incredibly sketch.
origin: California slang, widely adopted across Gen Z.

definition: (interjection) An immensely popular Spanglish exclamation translating roughly to "no way" or "you're kidding," expressing profound shock and disbelief.
exampleSentence: You actually won the lottery ticket? No mames, that is incredible!
origin: Los Angeles Spanglish / Chicano culture.

definition: (adj.) A Spanglish term used to describe an item, situation, or physical object that is incredibly cheap, broken, or of extremely low quality.
exampleSentence: I bought this charging cable at the gas station and it broke immediately, so chafa.
origin: Los Angeles Spanglish / Chicano culture.

definition: (n.) Meteorological slang referencing the dense, overcast marine layer clouds that persistently blanket the Southern California coastline during late spring and early summer.
standardEnglish: Marine layer overcast / Cloudy spring mornings
exampleSentence: We wanted to go to the beach early, but the June Gloom completely ruined the sunshine.
origin: Southern California regional meteorological slang.

definition: (n.) A unique geographic and syntactic identifier strictly native to Southern California, involving placing the definite article "the" before the numerical name of any highway.
exampleSentence: If you want to bypass the downtown traffic, you should take the 405 down to the 5.
origin: Southern California regional grammar quirk.

definition: (n.) Skull emoji entirely replacing the traditional "LOL." Indicates the user is "dead" from laughing excessively, or is completely overwhelmed by something shocking or embarrassing.
standardEnglish: I am dying of laughter / That killed me
exampleSentence: He slipped on a banana peel in front of everyone (skull emoji)
origin: Internet culture, replacing LOL as the dominant laughter indicator for Gen Z and Gen Alpha.

definition: (n.) Clown face emoji used aggressively to call out oneself or another person for acting deeply foolish, highly naive, or for believing an obvious lie.
standardEnglish: You are being foolish / I am an idiot
exampleSentence: I thought he actually liked me back (clown emoji)
origin: Internet culture, widely adopted by Gen Z as self-deprecating and insulting shorthand.

definition: (n.) Red flag emoji universally recognized as a warning sign. Indicates highly toxic behavior, a severe warning sign in a romantic relationship, or a problematic personality trait.
exampleSentence: He said he does not believe in apologizing (red flag emoji red flag emoji)
origin: Internet dating and relationship discourse, adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) Nail polish emoji conveying a distinct tone of sass, unbothered confidence, or extreme nonchalance. Often used to punctuate statements of high self-worth or to dismiss criticism.
standardEnglish: I do not care / With confidence
exampleSentence: I looked amazing at the party and they can be mad about it (nail polish emoji)
origin: Internet culture and drag / queer spaces, adopted broadly by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) Sparkles emoji used to frame text to indicate heavy sarcasm, mock-glamour, or to highlight something deeply mundane or tragic as if it were a magical event.
exampleSentence: (sparkles) woke up and immediately dropped my coffee (sparkles)
origin: Internet culture, evolved from decorative use to ironic framing by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) Billed cap emoji representing "cap" (a blatant lie or falsehood). Frequently used alongside the prohibition sign to explicitly indicate "no cap" or "no lie."
exampleSentence: He said he finished the project already (cap emoji)
origin: Internet culture derived from AAVE "cap," widely adopted by Gen Z.

definition: (n.) Upside-down face emoji conveying intense passive aggression, heavy sarcasm, internal screaming, or the act of accepting a disastrous situation with a forced smile.
standardEnglish: Passive aggression / Everything is fine (it is not fine)
exampleSentence: Got assigned a group project with the worst people in class (upside-down face emoji)
origin: Internet culture, adopted by Gen Z as the ultimate sarcasm and passive-aggression marker.

definition: (n.) Melting face emoji conveying a feeling of severe embarrassment, intense social anxiety, low mood, or melting away under extreme, unbearable pressure.
standardEnglish: I am embarrassed / I want to disappear
exampleSentence: My professor called on me and I completely blanked (melting face emoji)
origin: Unicode 14.0 (2021), rapidly adopted by Gen Z and Gen Alpha as a distress reaction.

definition: (n.) Shaking face emoji indicating extreme shock, total chaos, or the immediate aftermath of something highly dramatic and unsettling occurring.
standardEnglish: I am shaken / That is shocking
exampleSentence: The finale twist was insane, I am still (shaking face emoji)
origin: Unicode 15.0 (2022), adopted by Gen Z and Gen Alpha as a shock reaction.

definition: (n.) Heart hands emoji representing deep appreciation, highly positive vibes, or a supportive, affectionate comment delivered without explicit romantic undertones.
standardEnglish: I appreciate you / Love and support
exampleSentence: She always shows up when I need her most (heart hands emoji)
origin: Unicode 14.0 (2021), widely adopted by Gen Z for expressing warmth and support.

definition: (adj.) Describes highly advanced AI systems or workflows that possess the autonomy to independently plan, make decisions, and execute complex multi-step tasks with minimal human oversight.
exampleSentence: We are officially moving our entire data pipeline toward a fully agentic research model.
origin: Tech industry / AI engineering, 2025-2026.

definition: (n.) A derogatory term describing mass-produced, extremely low-quality, and frequently hallucinatory synthetic content generated by AI that heavily pollutes search engines and social media feeds.
exampleSentence: The search results for that historical event are completely buried under pages of AI-slop.
origin: Tech industry / general internet, emerging 2025-2026.

definition: (v.) A highly collaborative, iterative approach to software development where a programmer casually guides an AI coding assistant using natural language prompts, focusing on aesthetic flow rather than raw syntax.
exampleSentence: I spent the entire afternoon vibe coding a new landing page interface alongside the language model.
origin: Software engineering community, 2025-2026.

definition: (n.) Generative Engine Optimization. The direct evolutionary successor to traditional SEO; the practice of formatting structured data so AI models and LLMs are statistically more likely to retrieve and cite it.
exampleSentence: Our marketing agency is entirely pivoting away from standard search algorithms to focus strictly on GEO.
origin: Tech marketing / AI industry, 2025-2026.

definition: (n.) Used as a mass noun to describe the raw physical processing power (specifically GPUs and TPUs) required to train and run massive AI models. The primary geopolitical currency of the modern tech landscape.
exampleSentence: The primary reason we cannot scale the language model faster is that we simply cannot secure enough compute.
origin: Tech industry / AI engineering.

definition: (n.) Individuals who firmly believe that unchecked, exponential AI development will inevitably lead to catastrophic global societal collapse or human extinction.
exampleSentence: The panel debate completely devolved into a shouting match between the tech optimists and the AI doomers.
origin: Tech industry / AI safety community.

definition: (n.) Highly lazy, poorly constructed, and grammatically terrible user input fed into an AI system, which inevitably results in equally poor and useless output.
exampleSentence: You cannot expect the model to generate a usable codebase if you just feed it three lines of promptslop.
origin: Tech industry / AI user community, 2025-2026.

definition: (v.) The arduous process of starting and growing a startup company utilizing only personal resources and revenue, strictly without accepting outside venture capital.
exampleSentence: We are bootstrapping the entire application until we hit ten thousand active monthly users.
origin: Silicon Valley startup culture.

definition: (n.) An acronym representing the most dominant technology monopolies: Facebook (Meta), Apple, Amazon, Netflix, and Google (Alphabet). Symbolizes elite tech employment.
standardEnglish: Big Tech companies / Top tech employers
exampleSentence: He spent five grueling months studying algorithmic questions just to land a junior role at a FAANG company.
origin: Silicon Valley culture, widely used in tech hiring circles.
